pair.title: Ask HN: How to solve the cold start problem for a two-sided marketplace?

story.title: Ask HN: How to solve the cold start problem for a two-sided marketplace?

comments.text: The sock puppet thing is overblown. Reddit's early traction came from Paul Graham and YC posting there, not fake accounts. What actually worked was Airbnb's approach: have founders do the work themselves. Brian Chesky literally went to New York and photographed every listing personally, then drove

kids.text: Fair point on the Reddit history. The Airbnb angle is what I'm actually leaning toward now, seems like there's no real shortcut around doing the grunt work yourself first. Going to start with the supply side (drivers) and manually match them to demand I can scrape together. Thanks for the

comments.text:
The Reddit thing is worth unpacking a bit more carefully. Graham and other YC folks did seed early discussion, sure, but the actual mechanism that scaled it was different: they explicitly encouraged users to submit content, and early moderators (often the founders themselves or close collaborators) were extremely active in seeding subreddits with posts and participation. It wasn't random sockpuppetry-it was strategic, visible participation by people who understood the community.

For a two-sided marketplace, the equivalent isn't pretending demand exists. It's doing the operational work yourself first to prove the loop closes. Zappos famously photographed shoes from local stores and shipped them while building their supplier network. Airbnb's founders photographed apartments themselves. Instacart had employees picking groceries before opening to real shoppers.

The "one person on one side" framing is clearer than "cheating"-you're not faking activity, you're demonstrating that the unit economics work. Show 10 real businesses that need delivery, then recruit real couriers to service them. That's not juice, that's proof. The visibility comes from real transactions, not phantom ones.

The real insight here is that you're not actually cheating, you're just doing the work that a matching algorithm would eventually do anyway. You're the algorithm until you're not.

The key is to pick the side with lower friction to acquire first. For a delivery marketplace, that's usually supply (drivers), not demand (businesses). Drivers can work whenever, businesses need reliability. So find 5-10 drivers willing to take gigs, then go convince a few restaurants or shops that they should use this service.

Once you have supply, demand becomes a sales problem, not a chicken-and-egg problem. One person can close 10 businesses in a month. One person cannot recruit 100 drivers in a month.

The visibility

kids.text: I tried this with a local courier service in Portland. Started by doing 20 deliveries myself on weekends, then hired two drivers I knew. Got three shops to commit once they saw consistent pickups. The hard part wasn't finding either side - it was the ops overhead of manual routing and payment tracking.

This is exactly right. The ops overhead is what kills most people before they even get traction. Did you end up automating the routing eventually, or did you stay manual? Curious whether the manual phase was weeks or months before it became unsustainable.

comments.text:
I built a local logistics platform a few years back and the cold start was brutal. We did exactly what you're describing: I personally made about 40 deliveries in the first month, just to have real data and real feedback. That visibility mattered way more than the product polish.

The key insight was treating the demand side as the constraint, not supply. We signed up 8 businesses first (coffee shops, small retailers) with a personal pitch. Once we had consistent orders, recruiting drivers became trivial. Posted one Craigslist ad and got 15 applicants in a week.

The sock puppet thing is noise. What actually moved the needle was showing up in person, doing the work yourself, and being able to say "we moved 30 packages this week" to

kids.text: This whole thread is just people swapping war stories about grinding it out manually instead of actually discussing whether the cold start problem is solvable at scale. The OP is asking for strategy and we're all basically saying "yeah just do the work yourself" which isn't really an answer, it's just describing the

This is exactly what I needed to hear. The "demand side as constraint" reframes how I've been thinking about this completely. I'm going to start with 5-8 businesses next week and do the deliveries myself for the first month like you did. Thanks for the concrete validation that this approach actually

I ran this exact playbook with a micro-mobility startup in 2019. Started with 6 local gyms, did all pickups myself for 6 weeks. Paul Graham has a great essay on this (http://paulgraham.com/ds.html) - the "do things that

I did something similar with a task marketplace in 2018, except I made the mistake of trying to build supply and demand simultaneously. Spent three months optimizing the app, launched to crickets.

What changed was shifting to manual matching for the first two months. I'd literally text task posters and workers directly, collect their feedback over calls, then manually coordinate jobs through SMS. It felt unscalable and ridiculous at the time, but that's exactly what gave me the product insight I was missing.

The Paul Graham essay nails it, but the part people skip over is how much you have to be willing to do things that don't scale. I spent 30+ hours a week doing what the app was supposed to do automatically. But I also got to watch exactly where the friction was. Workers wanted SMS updates, not push notifications. Businesses cared more about reliability than price. These weren't things I would have guessed from user interviews.

One thing I'd add to the playbook: pick your demand side from a

comments.text:
You're framing this as a cold start problem, but I think the real problem is positioning. The marketplace angle assumes you need both sides day one. What if you actually just need one side solving a real problem first?

Uber didn't launch as a two-sided marketplace. It launched as "we'll

kids.text:
This is exactly right. Uber's first market was just black car service paired with a dispatcher app. They had supply (car services already existed) and solved a specific pain point (booking a car vs calling). The two-sided network effects came later.

For a delivery marketplace, you could start by being the delivery person yourself, manually matching requests from a handful of businesses. Once you've validated

I did exactly this with a local delivery service in 2015. Started by hand-delivering 15-20 packages a day myself, took photos, got testimonials from the businesses. Once I had 30 regular clients, recruiting drivers became trivial because I could show them actual demand. Took

This reminds me of Airbnb's early days. They literally went door-to-door in New York taking professional photos of listings themselves, because hosts weren't doing it. Most people forget that part - founders doing the unglamorous work to bootstrap one side.

The gotcha in the "positioning" framing is that it assumes you have the luxury of picking which side to build first. But in delivery, both sides have switching costs. A driver won't sign up for a platform with no jobs. A business won't use a platform with no drivers. Uber got around this by having insane VC money to subsidize rides and pay drivers well above market rate until critical mass hit.

Without that capital cushion, you're stuck doing what the parent describes: manually operate one side yourself (you do deliveries, you recruit drivers, you get jobs from businesses) until the two-sided loop works on its own.

That's a really fair point. I've been so caught up in "marketplace" as the end state that I haven't thought enough about what the actual problem is first. Starting with just solving delivery for businesses, then recruiting drivers to fill that demand, feels way more grounded than trying to bootstrap both sides simultaneously. I

comments.text:
The sock puppet thing is way overblown. What actually worked for Reddit was that early adopters like Paul Graham and other YC folks posted there organically. For a marketplace though, you're right that one side needs to move first.

Airbnb's playbook is more instructive here. They went to New York, photographed apartments themselves, listed them, then manually matched hosts with travelers for the first few hundred transactions. No fake demand, just founder-level legwork on both sides until

kids.text: You're right about the founder legwork angle. I've been assuming I need to build the product first, then recruit. The Airbnb model is clearer-they solved the chicken-egg by being both sides manually. I think the Reddit correction is fair too; early organic adoption beats fake activity. Going

You're right that the founder legwork angle is more honest than I gave it credit for. The Airbnb playbook makes sense-they had product-market fit on both sides because they were literally the ones doing the work initially. I've been assuming I need to fake demand or bootstrap both simultaneously, but what you're describing is just... solving the real problem first.

My question is timing: how long did you let that manual matching phase run before moving to platform automation? Were there early signals (X transactions, Y repeat customers) that told you it was time to invest in matching algorithms, or was it more intuition?

I did exactly this for a local task marketplace in 2019. Started with maybe 20 residential clients in my city who needed odd jobs done – handyman stuff, moving help, that sort of thing. Spent two months basically being the tasker myself, taking jobs on nights and weekends, getting real feedback on what clients actually wanted. Pricing, timing, how they preferred communication.

Then I went to the contractor side. Posted on local Facebook groups, Craigslist, even stood outside Home Depot talking to people. But here's the thing – I had legitimate work to show them. Not hypothetical, not "join my platform and maybe get work." I could say "I have 5 jobs lined up next week, interested?" That made recruiting infinitely easier.

The fake activity angle always seemed backwards to me. What actually moves people is legitimacy. A contractor will take a chance on an unproven platform if you can show them real money from real clients on day one. A client will stick around if the contractor shows up and does good work. You need at least one side grounded in reality.

I did the Home Depot thing too, except it was a loading dock at a warehouse. Stood there for three Saturday mornings with a sign. Got maybe five people interested, hired two of them. One stuck around for six months, the other disappeared after week two. But that one person who stayed became my

comments.text:
This approach of manually building one side first really resonates with me. I've seen it work incredibly well for logistics startups that focus on a specific vertical before going horizontal.

The key insight here is that you're not actually trying to build a perfect two-sided marketplace initially. You're building a service business where you're the operator, and the platform is just tooling to scale what already works. Instacart did this too, especially in the early days before opening up to third-party shoppers. They were the ones doing the shopping, validating demand, understanding the pain points, then gradually shifting logistics to other people.

For the delivery/courier angle specifically, I'd lean hard into the demand side first. Get 50-100 businesses actually using you to move packages before you worry about recruitment messaging. Once you have concrete jobs on the board, drivers will actually show up because there's real work. Right now, a bare platform with no jobs is worthless to a potential driver. But a person who can say "hey, I've got 12
